---
title: "POPULATION INDICES, TRENDS, AND DISTRIBUTION OF BREEDING WATERBIRDS ON THE ARCTIC COASTAL PLAIN, ALASKA, 2007-2025"
subtitle: ""
author:
  name: HEATHER M. WILSON, DAVID E. SAFINE, CHARLES J. FROST, AND ERIK E. OSNAS
  email: heather_wilson@fws.gov, david_safine@fws.gov, charles_frost@fws.gov, and erik_osnas@fws.gov
  affiliation:
      name: U.S Fish and Wildlife Service
      department: Migratory Bird Management
      city: "Anchorage, AK 99503"
date: ""
bibliography: "bibliography.bib"
toc: false
lof: false
lot: false
fig-cap-location: bottom
tbl-cap-location: top
format:
  arxiv-pdf:
    runninghead: "Arctic Coastal Plain Aerial Breeding Population Survey Report 2007-2025"
    number_sections: false
    keep-tex: true  
    fig-width: 8
    fig-height: 5
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \setlength{\defaultaddspace}{0pt}
prefer-html: true
editor: source

embed-resources: true
#code below adds line numbers for easier editing
#linenumbers: true
execute:
  message: false
  warning: false
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
if(file.exists("ACP_report.RData")){
  library(tidyverse)
  library(AKaerial)
  library(kableExtra)
  library(tinytex)
  library(zoo)
  library(tmap)
  load("ACP_report.RData")
}else{
  source("ACP_report.R")
}
```

# ABSTRACT {.unnumbered}

The Arctic Coastal Plain Aerial Breeding Population Survey (ACP Survey) provides data on the distribution and relative abundance of 33 bird species (including 29 waterbird species) that nest in northern Alaska. Among those reported are species of conservation concern, including threatened spectacled (*Somateria fischeri*) and Steller's eiders (*Polysticta stelleri*), yellow-billed loons (*Gavia adamsii*), and snow geese (*Anser caerulescens*). This report focuses on results from the `r min(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`-`r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)` ACP surveys and presents population indices for all species from `r min(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`-`r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`. No surveys were conducted in 2020-2021 due to the COVID pandemic. In `r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`, the respective indicated total bird indices and their associated standard errors (SE) for spectacled eiders were `r formatC(round(SPEI$itotal[SPEI$Year==2025],0), big.mark=",", format="d")` (SE: `r formatC(round(SPEI$itotal.se[SPEI$Year==2025],0), big.mark=",", format="d")`), and for Steller's eiders were `r formatC(round(STEI$itotal[STEI$Year==2025],0), big.mark=",", format="d")` (SE: `r formatC(round(STEI$itotal.se[STEI$Year==2025],0), big.mark=",", format="d")`), while total bird indices for yellow-billed loons were `r formatC(round(YBLO$total[YBLO$Year==2025],0), big.mark=",", format="d")` (SE: `r formatC(round(YBLO$total.se[YBLO$Year==2025],0), big.mark=",", format="d")`), and for snow geese were `r formatC(round(SNGO$total[SNGO$Year==2025],0), big.mark=",", format="d")` (SE: `r formatC(round(SNGO$total.se[SNGO$Year==2025],0), big.mark=",", format="d")`). Long-term (`r min(SPEI$Year)`-`r max(SPEI$Year)`) mean annual population indices and geometric mean growth rates (with their associated SE or 95% credible intervals in parentheses) for these species were: spectacled eiders (indicated total birds) `r formatC(round(SPEIh,0), big.mark=",", format="d")` (SE: `r formatC(round(SPEIhse,0), big.mark=",", format="d")`), growth rate `r sppdf$Trend[sppdf$Species == "SPEI"]`; Steller's eiders (indicated total birds) `r formatC(round(STEIh,0), big.mark=",", format="d")` (SE: `r formatC(round(STEIhse,0), big.mark=",", format="d")`), growth rate `r sppdf$Trend[sppdf$Species == "STEI"]`; yellow-billed loons (total birds) `r formatC(round(YBLOh,0), big.mark=",", format="d")` (SE: `r formatC(round(YBLOhse,0), big.mark=",", format="d")`), growth rate `r sppdf$Trend[sppdf$Species == "YBLO"]`; and snow geese (total birds) `r formatC(round(SNGOh,0), big.mark=",", format="d")` (SE: `r formatC(round(SNGOhse,0), big.mark=",", format="d")`), growth rate `r sppdf$Trend[sppdf$Species == "SNGO"]`. Currently, our population indices do not account for incomplete detection or other sources of bias, although the model we used to estimate growth rate removes average observer effects. Our growth rate model indicated long-term (`r min(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`-`r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`) population decline for scaup (*Aythya marila* and *A. affinis*), spectacled eiders, king eiders (*Somateria spectabilis*), and jaeger species (*Stercorarius sp.*) with posterior probabilites \> 0.975, while snow geese showed a high probability (\> 0.975) of long-term population increase (up to 23% per year). Greater white-fronted geese (*Anser albifrons*), northern pintail (*Anas acuta*), long-tailed ducks (*Clangula hyemalis*), and Pacific (*Gavia pacifica*) and yellow-billed loons were relatively stable, with a posterior mean growth rate between 0.99 and 1.01 (95% CI bound to $\leq$ 5% increase or decrease). Posterior estimates for other species showed less evidence of long-term population change. \newline

## *Key Words* {.unnumbered}

Aerial breeding pair survey, Alaska, Arctic Coastal Plain, population index, growth rate, species occurence, spectacled eider *Somateria fischeri*, snow goose *Anser caerulescens*, Stellerâ€™s eider *Polysticta stelleri*, trend, waterfowl, waterbird, yellow-billed loon *Gavia adamsii*.

{{< pagebreak >}}

# INTRODUCTION {.unnumbered}

The Arctic Coastal Plain (ACP) is a vast area of Arctic lowland tundra in far northern Alaska important for many species of breeding birds [@Johnson2007; @Bart2013; @Amundson2019], especially waterbirds. Importantly, it is the only regularly used breeding area for the threatened population of Stellerâ€™s eiders [*Polysticta stelleri*: @USFWS2002] in North America, and one of only three breeding areas for threatened spectacled eiders [*Somateria fischeri*: @USFWS1996]. Additionally, the ACP hosts many other waterbird species of conservation concern, including yellow-billed loons (*Gavia adamsii*), and snow geese (*Anser caerulescens*).

Annual aerial surveys of waterbirds on the ACP have been conducted since 1986. Prior to 2007, two surveys with different timing and coverage [i.e., the Original ACP Survey, 1986â€“2006: @Brackney1993; @Mallek2007; and the North Slope Eider Survey, 1992â€“2006: @Larned2006] were used to monitor waterbirds on the ACP. In 2007, the two historical surveys were merged [@Stehn2013], and the amalgamated survey, called the â€śArctic Coastal Plain Aerial Breeding Population Survey", hereafter "ACP Surveyâ€ť [@Larned2008; @Larned2012; @Stehn2013; @Stehn2014; @Wilson2018; @Wilson2025] has been flown annually since 2007 (with the exception of 2020 and 2021, due to the COVID pandemic). The survey provides distribution and abundance information for 33 species of birds, including loons, swans, geese, ducks, gulls, jaegers, terns, and selected landbird species breeding in northern Alaska. These results provide population status and distribution information to the Pacific Flyway Council, the Alaska Migratory Bird Co-Management Council, spectacled and Stellerâ€™s eider Recovery Teams, U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service (USFWS), U.S. Geological Survey, Bureau of Land Management, North Slope Borough, various non-governmental organizations, local communities, consulting firms, and industry. This report presents updated population information for all 33 bird species monitored from `r min(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`-`r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`.

![Study area and survey design for the Arctic Coastal Plain Aerial Breeding Population Survey (ACP Survey) in northern Alaska, illustrating the 2025 transects, strata, and village locations. The exact locations of survey transects are offset each year based on a four-year rotating panel within four physiographic and bird-density-based strata (high, medium, low, and Teshekpuk high).](data/fig1.png){#fig-Fig1 fig-alt="Study area and survey design map for the Arctic Coastal Plain Aerial Breeding Population Survey (ACP Survey) in northern Alaska, illustrating the `r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)` transects, strata, and village locations. The exact locations of survey transects vary slightly each year according to a four-year rotating panel within four physiographic and bird-density-based strata (high, medium, low, and Teshekpuk high)."}

# METHODS {.unnumbered}

## Survey Design {.unnumbered}

The ACP Survey study area encompasses 57,227 km^2^, of tundra wetlands, extending from the Chukchi Sea coast in the west to the Canadian border in the east, and from the foothills of the Brooks Range to the Beaufort Sea. As flown since 2007, the ACP Survey is broken into four physiographic and bird-density-based strata, with a 4-year rotating panel of systematic strip-transects within each stratum (@fig-Fig1). Herein, the within-strata sampling effort is proportional to bird densities, such that fewer transects are surveyed in strata where bird densities are lower. The four-year rotating panel design results in the panel (i.e., group) of transects being shifted by 25% of the inter-transect distance each year. 

The annual inter-transect distances for each of the four ACP Survey strata are 4.8 km (Teshekpuk High), 9.6 km (High), 19.2 km (Medium), and 28.8 km [Low; @Larned2012, @fig-Fig1]. Given the 400 m transect width, this results in average annual sampling fractions of \~8.3%, 4.2%, 2.1%, and 1.3%, in the respective strata. Thus, within a 4-year panel of transects, the inter-transect distances for each of the four ACP Survey strata are 1.2 km (Teshekpuk High), 2.4 km (High), 4.8 km (Medium), and 7.2 km (Low), resulting in sampling fractions of \~33.2%, 16.7%, 8.5%, and 5.2%, in the respective strata over a four-year period. Total areas of each stratum are Teshekpuk High: 5,655 km^2^, High: 20,258 km^2^, Medium: 13,048 km^2^, and Low: 18,266 km^2^.


## Survey Methods {.unnumbered}

The ACP Survey methodology is based on standard operating procedures for the Waterfowl Breeding Population and Habitat Survey (USFWS and CWS 1987), with both the left-front seat biologist-pilot and right-front seat observer recording all waterfowl, loons, gulls, jaegers, terns, eagles, owls, and ravens seen within a strip transect encompassing 200 m on either side of the flight path. To estimate the outer transect boundary, crews determine the required viewing angle trigonometrically and mark a reference point on the wing strut and side window for each observer, using a clinometer and marking tape/pens. Surveys are flown at an approximate ground speed of 161 km/hr (\~87 kts), and an altitude of 38 m (125 ft) above ground level (AGL), as referenced by a radar altimeter installed in the aircraft. Georeferenced observations made from both sides of the aircraft are voice-recorded into panel-mounted devices for later transcription using the custom software packages RECORD and TRANSCRIBE [2007-2022: @Hodges2015Record] and H2 and SCRIBE [2023-present: @H2]. All survey years, except 2011 and 2012, were flown with a Cessna 206 amphibious-equipped aircraft (Cessna Aircraft Company, Wichita, KS) and only two biologist-pilot observers flew the survey in all years (Larned [2007-2011] and Wilson [2012-`r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`]); @tbl-crew). The `r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)` survey required 46 flight-hours to complete, not including ferry time (approximately 15 hours round-trip) from Anchorage.

```{r table1, include=TRUE, echo=FALSE}
#| label: tbl-crew
cap <- paste0 ("Arctic Coastal Plain Aerial Breeding Population Survey (ACP Survey) dates and crews (",min(ACPHistoric$combined$Year),"-",max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year),"). The current ACP Survey combines the timing of the historical North Slope Eider Survey (1992-2006) with the geographic coverage of the Original ACP Survey (1986-2006), and has been flown since 2007. No surveys were flown in 2020 or 2021 due to the COVID pandemic. Aircraft used were either amphibious-equipped Cessna 206 (C206) or Kodiak 100.")

kable(table1, digits = 0, booktabs = TRUE, linesep = "", longtable=TRUE, caption=cap, align = "ccccc", col.names = c("Year", "Data Collection", "Left-front observer", "Right-front observer", "Aircraft")) %>%
  kable_styling(font_size=10, position="center") %>%
  add_header_above(bold = TRUE, c(" "=1, "Dates of" = 1, "Pilot" = 1, "Non-pilot" = 1, " " = 1)) %>%
  row_spec(row = 0, bold = TRUE) 

```

## Survey Timing and Weather Conditions {.unnumbered}

Timing of survey initiation is intended to coincide with the egg laying/early incubation period of nesting geese and eiders on the ACP, and the peak presence of male ducks and pairs of other waterfowl species. This is a period when nesting habitat is just becoming available (i.e., ice-free water is visible in most shallow vegetated wetlands, and tundra vegetation is mostly snow-free), typically within the first three weeks of June. According to @Troy1997, median nest initiation dates for spectacled eiders at Prudhoe Bay averaged 15 June from 1982 to 1996, with males departing within 3â€“5 days of median initiation. More recent data from Utqiagvik, estimated average spectacled eider nest initiation to be 14 June [@Safine2013], consistent with indicated average (5-year; 2017-2019, 2021-2022) nest initiation for cackling geese on the Canning River Delta (14 June; C. Latty, personal communication, Feb. 11, 2025). Within the first two weeks of June each year (@tbl-crew), we refined our survey start dates based on close monitoring of weather and temperature data, examining snow and ice-cover changes via satellite ([NASA Worldview](https://worldview.earthdata.nasa.gov/)) and web-camera imagery (<https://weathercams.faa.gov//>), and by receiving updates on current landscape conditions from biologists and other local residents on the ACP.  Weather conditions varied considerably within and among survey years. Reduced visibility and ceilings due to coastal fog were the most consistent weather impediments to surveying in all years of the survey. Fog was particularly troublesome along the northern coastal fringe of the study area, where on-shore winds (blowing over sea ice), small temperature-dew point spreads, and daily temperature cycles could create instantaneous, low-visibility conditions.

## Population Indices {.unnumbered}

We calculated population indices to be consistent with their use in other USFWS surveys. Duck indices followed the guidance of @USFWSandCWS1987, and goose, swan, and crane indices followed @Eldridge2003. Pairs were defined as the total number of male-female pairs (or two monomorphic birds \[e.g., geese\] in close association), not the total number of birds in pairs [@USFWSandCWS1987]. Flocked drakes were defined as the total number of males observed with one or more other males and no female present [@USFWSandCWS1987], and were only recorded for ducks. Flocked drakes in groups of less than 5 birds were doubled (except for scaup; see below), and flocked drakes in groups of 5 or more were treated as a flock and not doubled [@USFWSandCWS1987]. Flocks were defined as a closely associated single-sex or mixed-sex grouping of 5 or more birds (or 2 or more birds of different species) that could not be separated into singles and pairs. For scaup, single drakes and flocked drakes in groups of less than 5 birds were not doubled as they were for other ducks. This is because sex ratios in scaup lean heavily towards males, such that not all males can be assumed to have a female mate [@USFWSandCWS1987]. The breeding birds index for scaup has been corrected to include flocked drakes in groups of less than 5 birds for all survey years (`r min(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`-`r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`). Formulas for the calculation of the four population indices presented in this report are shown below.

For dimorphic species (e.g., ducks \[except scaup\]) and some monomorphic species (cranes and dark geese \[e.g., greater white-fronted geese, cackling/Canada geese, and brant\]):

```{=tex}
\begin{center}

\textit{Indicated Breeding Birds = 2(singles + pairs + flocked drakes in groups of < 5 birds)} \newline

\textit{Indicated Total Birds = 2(singles + pairs + flocked drakes in groups of < 5 birds) + birds in flocks} \newline

\end{center}

For the remainder of the monomorphic species (e.g., swans, snow geese, grebes, loons, terns, gulls, jaegers, owls, eagles, ravens) and scaup species:\newline

\begin{center}

\textit{Breeding Birds = singles + 2(pairs) + flocked drakes in groups of < 5 birds} \newline

\textit{Total Birds = singles + 2(pairs) + flocked drakes in groups of < 5 birds + birds in flocks} \newline

\end{center}
```
## The Ratio Estimator {.unnumbered}

Population totals for each index were calculated using a ratio estimator [@Cochran1977] with transects as the sample units. The classical ratio estimator [@Cochran1977] is a straightforward approach that can be used to estimate the population total of observed birds in a survey area given a systematic or random sampling design with large sample size (i.e., number of transects). The ratio estimator is especially good when the response (i.e., number of birds on a transect) is linearly related to the length or area of the transect. We employed a stratified strip-transect design for the majority of our surveys of breeding waterfowl. Study areas are separated into spatial strata based on physiographic features and historical bird densities. Strata were sampled by low-level flights along a series of strip transects, with one or two observers searching 200 meters out from the transect center. Observations and sampled areas are then summed across transects within each stratum to produce strata-specific density estimates. These densities are then multiplied by associated strata areas to produce stratum-specific population indices that are summed to produce the overall population indices, $$E[\hat Y] = \displaystyle\sum_{i}^{S} \frac{\bar y_i}{\bar a_i} A_i = \displaystyle\sum_{i}^{S} \hat D_i A_i$$ where $y_i$ are observations and $a_i$ are sampled areas in strata $i$, and $$A_i = \displaystyle\sum_{j}^{M_i} a_{ij}$$ with estimated variance from Williams et al. (2002), where standard error (SE) is calculated based on inter-transect variance,Â  $$Var(\hat Y) = \displaystyle\sum_{i}^{S} M_i^2 \frac{(1-m_i / M_i)}{m_i} ( s^2_{iy} + \hat {D^2}_i s^2_{ia} - 2 \hat {D_i} s_{iay} ),$$ where $m_i$ and $M_i$ are the number of sampled and total transects in strata $i$, respectively, and there are $S$ total strata;Â  $$S^2_{ix} = \displaystyle\sum_{j}^{m_i} (x_{ij} - \bar x )/(m_i -1), $$ andÂ  $$S^2_{ixy} = \displaystyle\sum_{j}^{m_i} (x_{ij} - \bar x )(y_{ij} - \bar y )/(m_i -1).$$ The equations above are observer-specific (i.e., calculated for each observer independently). However, the estimates presented in this report are the arithmetic mean of observer-specific estimates (n=2, in most years). Design transects (@fig-Fig1) were used to calculate survey effort, but flight lines sometimes deviated slightly from the these due to weather avoidance or other factors. Data manuplulation was completed using the R [@R] package `AKaerial` [@r-akaerial].

Population indices presented here do not account for incomplete detection, though there are efforts to incorporate detection data into annual population estimates for some species [@Wilson2017; @Osnas2026]. Summary statistics were calculated for the indicated total bird (or total bird) index for each bird species, including the long-term (`r min(SPEI$Year)`- `r max(SPEI$Year)`) and most recent 3-year (`r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year) - 2`-`r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`) averages and their standard errors (@tbl-results). Indicated breeding bird (or breeding bird) indices and indicated total bird (or total bird) indices for each bird species are presented in the Appendices (@tbl-SNGO - @tbl-CORA and @fig-SNGO - @fig-CORA). Throughout, we report point estimates $\pm$ (1.96 x SE) as a 95% confidence interval, except in the simulations for trends (see next section).

## Index Trends {.unnumbered}

Trajectories in population indices were estimated using generalized additive models [GAM, @wood2017] in the R package `mgcv` [@r-mgcv] fit to observer-specific index estimates. The GAM model used a scaled-t likelihood, a continuous smooth term for year, and a random effect for observer in the linear predictor. The observer effect allowed us to remove observer-specific effects from any estimated average trajectory, and the scaled-t likelihood allowed for extra residual variance from the trend line over a normal likelihood that might be due to additional year-specific variance from the trend line. To account for uncertainty in the index estimate, we used a parametric bootstrap of the estimated index as the modeled response in the GAM. Thus, for each year, a response was sampled from a normal distribution (with the mean and standard deviation of the point estimate), truncated to a small value (1e-10) for samples $\leq$ 0, and log-transformed. Then a GAM was fit, and a sample of the parameters was obtained from a multivariate normal distribution using the estimated parameter vector and covariance matrix. Annual predictions over the full time series were made from this parameter sample after removing the effect of observer, and the results were saved. This procedure was repeated 300 times. As such, this procedure is an empirical Bayesian method to approximate the posterior distribution of the trajectory [@miller2025bayesian]. For rarer species, some of which were not observed in all years (e.g., red-necked grebes and Steller's eiders), we excluded years when no birds were observed. We did this because observed zeros in these design-based estimates are more likely due to non-detection or random sampling, rather than complete absence of the species in the survey area, and models fit to data that included zeros did not produce stable estimates. However, we acknowledge that this may have resulted in some over-smoothing and bias of estimated trends for species with zero-count years. For improved methods, that better account for zero observations in rare species see [@Amundson2019, @Osnas2026].

{{< pagebreak >}}

We summarized the simulated yearly GAM predictions (i.e., the posterior distribution of trajectories) by calculating the mean and standard deviation for each year. An example of simulated results and observer-specific estimates for one species (Pacific loon) is shown in @fig-loon. For plots and tables presented in the Results and Appendices, we do not report the observer-specific index estimates or individual samples of the smooths, in an effort to reduce clutter. Instead, we show only the average index between observers in any year, the posterior mean trajectory, and $\pm$ 2 standard errors from this mean.

![An example of index estimates and modeled trend results for Pacific loon (*Gavia pacifica*), accounting for observer effects. Colored points and bars are observer-specific index estimates, which were sampled 300 times and used as the response in a generalized additive model. Individal modeled predictions for each sample are shown by the thin gray lines. These make up the posterior distribution of modeled trajectories and are summarized by the orange band (approximate 95% credible interval) and the thick gray line (posterior mean). End points of the modeled predictions (thin gray lines) are used to calculate the posterior distribution of the long-term growth rate.](data/PALO_index_2.png){#fig-loon width="80%" height="40%" fig-alt="A figure showing observer-specific estimate for Pacific loon and 300 posterior samples of the modeled GAM trajectories, the posterior mean trajectory, and the approximate 95% confidence interval on the trajectory."}

We estimated the long-term average growth rate from year $a$ to $b$ for each simulated replicate as

$$T_{b-a} = (\hat N_{b}/\hat N_{a})^{1/(b-a)},$$


where $\hat N_t$ is the predicted index value at year $t$ from the bootstrap replicate GAM model, $t = a$ is the first year of a non-zero estimate, and $t = b$ is the last year. Thus, the growth rate was calculated as the geometric mean annual change in the model-predicted index. We summarized growth rate by the mean and 95% credible interval across the sampled replicates. We also calculated the posterior probability of a negative growth rate by summarizing the proportion of posterior samples that were \< 1 (Pr\[Trend \< 1\], @tbl-results). We only calculated growth rate for total or indicated total bird indices shown in @tbl-results. Code is available in the file `plot_trends_design.R` at [@osnasgithub].

## Relative Density {.unnumbered}

Following the methods of @Amundson2019 as updated by @Osnas2026, we used generalized additive models [GAMs, @wood2017] to estimate density surfaces from segementized transect data. Briefly, transects were broken up into 6 km long segements and bird observations, including observations of zero birds, were assigned to each transect segment. A set of GAMs with spatial, temporal, spatio-temporal smooth effects and observer and year random effects were then fit to the data for each species separately [see @Osnas2026 for details]. AIC was used to select the best model for each species, and then this model was used to make predictions at 1 km^2^ cells across the ACP. Importantly, predictions were made after removing the effects of observer, year, and the spatio-temporal (year-location) interaction and did not correct for any detection process. Thus, predictions represent relative variation in density across space after averaging across years and observers.

## Important Bird Areas {.unnumbered}

We used the density surfaces to compute a measure of the relative importance of location to breeding waterbirds across the ACP. For each 1 km^2^ prediction, we standardized each species' density by subtracting the minimum density across the ACP for that species and dividing by the range of density. We then summed this across a set of 20 species (those species in @Amundson2019). Therefore, the relative importance for each cell $i$ across all species indexed by $s$ was 

$$RI_i = \sum_s \frac{(d_{i,s} - min(d_s))}{max(d_s)-min(d_s)},$$
where $d_{i,s}$ is the relative density in cell $i$ for species $s$. We then plotted this raster across the survey area. 

# RESULTS {.unnumbered}

## Survey Timing and Weather Conditions {.unnumbered}

Dates of data collection for `r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)` were 16-24 June (@tbl-crew). Survey timing in `r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)` was similar to the latest on record for the survey in 2018 (15-25 June; @tbl-crew). Similar to 2018, in `r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`, sustained cold temperatures, strong east winds, and significant snow and ice covered much of the northern ACP landscape well into June. During the `r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)` survey, we observed significant flooding along the banks and at the mouths of the major rivers (e.g., Sagavanirktok, Colville, Ikpikpuk), resulting in flooded nesting areas for coastal-fringe nesting species, with particular disruption to colonial nesters such as brant and snow geese. We observed flooded nesting areas (with most land underwater), floating nests, and large flocks of flying geese in these areas.

## Population Indices and Relative Density {.unnumbered}

We summarize the most recent 3-year average (`r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year) - 2`-`r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`) and long-term average (`r min(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`-`r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`) population indices, long-term (`r min(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`-`r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`) posterior mean trajectories (trends) and associated 95% credible intervals (CI), as well as posterior probabilities that trends are decreasing (Pr[Trend \< 1]) for 33 bird species observed on the ACP Survey (@tbl-results). Maps of the most recent year's individual species-observations and long-term expected density maps for each species are also presented in the Appendices (@fig-SNGOmap - @fig-CORAmap). GAM model fit objects (in R package `mgcv::gam` result objects), model summaries and diagnostics (in a human-readable html document), and raster data (in geotiff format) for average relative densities across all years is available for each species in an accompanying data release [@ACPmapping2026].  Herein, we follow the taxonomic ordering of @Chesser2024. For each species, we also present individual annual population indices (`r min(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`-`r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`) and trajectories in tabular and graphical formats (Appendices: @tbl-SNGO - @tbl-CORA and @fig-SNGO - @fig-CORA). 

```{r table2, include=TRUE, echo=FALSE}
#| label: tbl-results

#TABLE 2 - Creates a summary table with a row of estimates for each species

cap <- paste0 ("The most recent 3-year (",(max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year) - 2), "-",max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year),") and long-term (",min(ACPHistoric$combined$Year),"-",max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year),") average population indices and associated standard errors (SE), as well as long-term annual growth rates (long-term trend), associated 95% credible intervals (CI), and posterior probability that a trend is decreasing (Pr[Trend < 1]) for bird species on the Arctic Coastal Plain Aerial Breeding Population Survey in Alaska.  Index formulas used are species-specific, with indicated total birds (ITB) used for all dimorphic species except scaup (*Aythya marila* and *A. affinis*), plus greater white-fronted geese (*Anser albifrons*), brant (*Branta bernicla nigricans*), cackling/Canada geese (*Branta hutchinsii* and *B. canadensis*), and sandhill cranes (*Antigone canadensis*); and total birds (TB) used for all other species. See the Methods section for detailed index definitions and trend calculations. Abundance estimates are not corrected for incomplete detection, but trends do account for observer effects.")

kable(table2, digits = 0, booktabs = TRUE, longtable=TRUE, format.args = list(big.mark = ","), 
      caption=cap, align = "llrrrrcc", col.names = c("Species", "Index", "Average", "SE", 
                                                     "Average", "SE", "Trend (CI)", "Pr(Trend < 1)"), 
      linesep = "") %>%
  kable_styling(font_size=10, position="center") %>%
  add_header_above(bold = TRUE, c(" "=1, " "=1, "3-Year" = 1, " "=1, "Long-term" = 1, 
                                  " "=1,"Long-term"=1, " "=1)) %>%
  row_spec(row = 0, bold = TRUE)

```

## Important Bird Areas {.unnumbered}

Areas of highest relative importance to 20 selected species of breeding waterbirds include the Colville River Delta and Nuiqsut, areas immediately south of Admiralty Bay, and areas south of Utqiagvik (@fig-Import). Other areas of high relative importance include areas near Utqiagvik, Atqasuk, the central NPR-A, areas surrounding Teshekpuk Lake, coastal areas northwest of Deadhorse, the Canning River Delta, and areas between Point Lay and Wainwright. 

```{r import, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
#| label: fig-Import
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 2
#| fig-cap: Relative breeding waterbird habitat importance across the Arctic Coastal Plain, Alaska based on average density (indicated breeding pairs per square kilometer) from 2007 to 2025 of the 20 species reported in Amundson et al. (2019). 
#| fig-alt: Relative breeding waterbird habitat importance across the Arctic Coastal Plain, Alaska based on average density (indicated breeding pairs per square kilometer) from 2007 to 2025 of the 20 species reported in Amundson et al. (2019). 
library(terra)
library(sf)
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyterra)
r <- rast(x="data/gam/Rel_import.tif")
p <- ggplot() +
  geom_spatraster(data = r) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "magma", na.value = "transparent", 
                       name = "Density") + 
  coord_sf(crs = 3338) + 
  #guides(fill=guide_legend(title="Density")) +
  theme(legend.position = "top", 
        panel.grid.major = element_blank(), 
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), 
        panel.background = element_blank(), 
        panel.border = element_rect(colour = "black", 
                                    fill = NA, linewidth = 1),
        plot.margin = margin(0, 0, 0, 0, "pt"), 
        legend.margin = margin(0, 0, 0, 0),
        legend.box.spacing = unit(0, "pt"))
p
ggsave("data/fig3.png", width = 7, height=2)
```

# DISCUSSION {.unnumbered}

This report describes indices and trends in relative abundance for all common waterbirds (excluding shorebirds; due to their small size and inconsistent data collection), owls, eagles, and ravens on the ACP of Alaska, collected by USFWS from `r min(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`-`r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`. For most species, 2025 index estimates were similar to those in the previous year.  However, for snow geese and brant, total bird indicies were drastically higher in 2025; a result likely related to significant coastal flooding of several high density nesting areas for these species, and more flocked birds, than breeding pairs and singles observed as a consequence. Our population growth analyses demonstrated high confidence in population decreases for scaup, spectacled eiders, king eiders, and jaeger species (posterior probability that the long-term trend (2007â€“`r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`) was decreasing was \> 0.975). The decreasing trend of spectacled eiders likely indicates that this species is further from meeting recovery criteria than it was 5 years ago when the population status was evaluated in @Dunham2021. With less, but still relatively high confidence (posterior probability that the long-term trend was decreasing \> 0.80), tundra swan (0.92), mallard (0.94), red-necked grebes (0.83), glaucous gull (0.91), Arctic tern (0.84), and common raven (0.97) all showed some evidence for a decreasing trend (@tbl-results). In contrast, snow geese showed a high probability (\> 0.975) of long-term population increase. The increasing trend of snow geese on the ACP is a conservation concern due to the potential for long-term habitat alternation in the breeding colonies that may affect other bird species (e.g., brant; @Hupp2017). With less, but still relatively high confidence (posterior probability \> 0.80), brant (0.88), Sabineâ€™s gull (0.91), red-throated loon (0.86), and short-eared owl (0.82) all showed some evidence for an increasing trend (@tbl-results). Greater white-fronted geese, northern pintail, long-tailed ducks, and Pacific and yellow-billed loons were relatively stable, with posterior mean growth rates between 0.99 and 1.01 (95% credible intervals bound to $\leq$ 5% increase or decrease), while posterior estimates for other species showed less evidence for long-term population change (@tbl-results).

Though observer bias is a factor in all aerial surveys, the experience-level and stability of the ACP Survey crew, particularly the left-seat pilot-observers, has been remarkably high. From `r min(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`-`r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`, there were only two pilot-observers who collected left-seat data (Larned [2007-2011] and Wilson [2012-`r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`]) and viewed together, seats occupied by Larned and Wilson represented one third of all data collected over the duration of the survey (`r min(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`-`r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`; @tbl-crew). Given this stability in observer-personnel, the estimated trends will be unbiased if there is little variation in detection or other observation processes within observers across years. We did, however, use a model to statistically remove average observer effects from the estimated average trajectory. This represents an improvement over historical calculations of long-term trend estimates, and lacking any trend in detection or other observation biases, the trends reported here (with the exception of Steller's eiders, see @Osnas2026) are the best currently available for this area without directly estimating observer biases. Nevertheless, detection and availability biases are still largely unaccounted for, affecting our population indices. Thus, these estimate are not measures of absolute abundance. Though we do not provide detection-adjusted population estimates for `r min(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`-`r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`, we did initiate a study examining observer detection of eiders and other waterbirds on the ACP in 2015 [@Wilson2017] and hope to incorporate detection and observer-adjusted estimates into future reports, using methods similar to @Osnas2026 for Steller's eiders.

The trajectories estimated here represent continuous smooth functions after removing average observer effects. We did not fit other models that allowed for more complex or discontinuous trends to these design-based index estimates, such as year-specific random effects or spatially explicit predictions [e.g. @smith2021north; @Osnas2026]. Because many species do show large year-specific deviations from an average trend, a model with such effects is likely a better description for these species (as evidence by the results used for spatial mapping or in @Osnas2026). Such models are not well-estimated when fit to the design-based estimates used here. The model we have used, however, can be viewed as a flexible way to estimate the average trajectory through time, smoothing over year-specific variations. Compared to a simple log-linear regression on point estimates, the current approach provides for more flexible functional forms of the trajectory, allowing for the identification of cycles or periods of change. We encourage the reader to keep these points in mind when interpreting results, as for many species, the year-specific variations are much larger than the average change over time (e.g., northern pintail) or show cyclical patterns over a shorter time (e.g., long-tailed duck, yellow-billed loon).  

Trajectories for rare species (those with observed zero estimates) should be interpreted with care. We attempted to fit our model by including zero estimates (after adding a small number and log transforming), but the GAM often failed to find stable estimates, so we used only positive estimates. This will have two important consequences. First, trajectories may be biased if there is a trend in the frequency of zero observations in the time series. Second, the GAM will tend to produce smoother trajectories than if the zeros were included. For example, the Steller's eider trajectory is essentially a flat line (@fig-STEI); whereas @Osnas2026 found clear cyclical patterns in the trajectory when a different GAM model was fit to smaller-scale, spatially-explicit counts that included zero-counts. Interestingly, @Amundson2019 also excluded zero-counts in a spatially-explicit GAM, and found a flat trajectory for Steller's eider over a much longer time period; suggesting that the smooth GAM is due to a reduction in data when most observations are excluded. We expect similar patterns for other rare species. However, for more common species that do not include zero estimates, we believe the current model should adequately approximate temporal trajectories in relative abundance.

Several regions of the ACP were identified as important, high-density bird areas (@fig-Import); specifically the Colville River Delta and Nuiqsut area, areas immediately south of Admiralty Bay, and areas south of Utqiagvik. In general, higher waterbird densities were observed near the coast, river deltas, near Teshekpuk Lake, and the central NPR-A. Overall, these results are similar to those of @Amundson2019, despite our methods for mapping density and constructing relative importance indices being different. Some species were widespread and occurred at higher densities across the ACP (e.g., greater white-fronted geese, northern pintail, long-tailed ducks, and Pacific loons; @fig-GWFGmap B, @fig-NOPImap B, @fig-LTDUmap B, @fig-PALOmap B).  Whereas, other species had more localized distributions and often occurred at lower densities (e.g., Steller's eiders, white-winged scoters, and snowy owls; @fig-STEImap B, @fig-WWSCmap B, @fig-SNOWmap B). For the least abundant species, like black scoters or northern shovelers, the density maps should not be over-interpreted given the paucity of observations for these species (@fig-BLSCmap B, @fig-NSHOmap B).      

Although the long-standing, continental-scale aerial Waterfowl Breeding Population and Habitat Survey samples most of Alaskaâ€™s primary waterfowl production areas, it has never included the rich wetlands of the Arctic Coastal Plain of Alaska or the high-Arctic of Canada. Further, while several targeted monitoring programs for specific species or limited areas of the ACP have been conducted; to our knowledge, the ACP Survey represents the most comprehensive aerial survey of Arctic-breeding waterbirds in existence. The analysis and implementation of such a multi-species, aerial monitoring program is challenging due to the inherent variability of the natural ecosystem, the varied natural histories of 30+ species, and the logistical difficulties of conducting aerial surveys in the Arctic. Moreover, it is difficult to achieve adequate within-year sampling of such a large spatial area within a short annual phenological window. Surveys of this type are further complicated by several waterfowl species that exist in extremely low densities and/or breed irregularly (such as Stellerâ€™s eiders), making precise estimates and trends difficult to achieve [though see @Osnas2026]. Collection of such a long-term data set has been made possible by integrating improvements in sampling design along the way and the long-term support of various partners. The sample design used in this survey was originally developed in 1986 to target breeding ducks [Original ACP Survey 1986-2006: @Mallek2007], and later augmented to better include coastal areas and earlier-nesting species, such as eiders [North Slope Eider Survey 1992â€“2006: @Larned2008]. The current, redesigned survey [2007â€“present: @Stehn2013; @Wilson2025] amalgamates these two designs to provide a good temporal, spatial, and inferential compromise. However, the changing distributions and abundances of many waterfowl species, as well as the rapid and wide-spread landscape changes in the Arctic, may warrant reevaluation of the current stratification and overall survey design. Nonetheless, this survey represents one of few broad-scale, long-term, systematic monitoring efforts for waterbirds in the Arctic of North America, and perhaps, the world.

# SUPPLEMENTAL MATERIAL {.unnumbered}

Species-specific population indices, trends, and spatial distributions of observations can be found in the tables and figures of the Appendices. Original data for the ACP Survey can be found at Science Base - Alaska Arctic Coastal Plain Breeding Waterbird Aerial Survey 2007-Present <https://doi.org/10.7944/f6jd-2985> and point-estimates (and documentation of their calculation) were sourced from the R package AKaerial at <https://github.com/USFWS/AKaerial>. The R and Quarto code used to produce this report can be found at <https://github.com/USFWS/ak-waterfowl-reports/tree/main/ACP_Report>. The report and associated tabular data can be found at <https://doi.org/10.7944/epgm-wt76>.

# SUGGESTED CITATION {.unnumbered}

For this report: 

Wilson, H.M., Safine, D.E., Frost, C.J., and E.E. Osnas. `r format(Sys.Date(), "%Y")`. Population indices, trends, and distribution of breeding waterbirds on the Arctic Coastal Plain, Alaska, 2007-`r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`. US Fish and Wildlife Service, Division of Migratory Bird Management, Anchorage, Alaska. <https://doi.org/10.7944/epgm-wt76>.

For the accompanying data release of density rasters and model results: 

Osnas, E.E. 2026. Spatial and temporal trends of waterbirds on the Arctic Coastal Plain, Alaska, using generalized additive models: data release. US Fish and Wildlife Service, Division of Migratory Bird Management, Anchorage, Alaska. <https://doi.org/10.7944/dy8h-z352>. 

# ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS {.unnumbered}

This survey would not be possible without the generous support of the Bureau of Land Management, Ducks Unlimited, the Alaska Department of Fish and Game, and the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Serviceâ€™s Northern Alaska Fish and Wildlife Field Office. We also extend thanks to Bill Larned for his many years flying and mentoring on the survey, Michael Swaim for assistance with aviation survey and hazard maps, and Julian Fischer for his thoughtful report reviews, project support, and program leadership. We are grateful to the people of Atqasuk, Alaska for hosting us in their community for so many years, and for helping with logistics and housing. Finally, we acknowledge the tremendous contribution of the observers and pilots who collected aerial waterbird data on the ACP beginning in 1986 [@Mallek2007; @Stehn2013], and from 2007 forward (@tbl-crew); and all those who supported this survey.

<center>

![Arctic Coastal Plain Aerial Breeding Population Survey Crew 2025: Heather Wilson (left-front seat observer/pilot) and Wade Schock (right-front seat observer)](data/Crew_picture_Wilson_Shock_ACP_2025.jpg){fig-alt="Photo of the Arctic Coastal Plain Aerial Breeding Population Survey (ACP Survey) Crew `r max(ACPHistoric$combined$Year)`. Heather Wilson (left-front seat observer/pilot) and Wade Schock (right-front seat observer) stand next to the amphibious-equipped Cessna 206 survey aircraft N375F in Atqasuk, Alaska." width="50%"
  fig-align="center"
}


# LITERATURE CITED {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::

{{< pagebreak >}}

{{< include _appendices.qmd >}}
